with_agent_vs_baseline.pass_rate: +23% (100% vs 77%) — baseline produces equally strong analysis but different schema
with_agent_vs_baseline.tokens: +6% more tokens (35K vs 33K — minimal overhead for reading instructions)
with_agent_vs_baseline.tool_uses: +14% more (33 vs 29 — agent runs additional validation queries)
with_agent_vs_baseline.quality: Both configs produce HIGH-quality cross-referencing analysis. The baseline actually found MORE findings in some cases (10 vs 5 for YEMANITY, 8 vs 7 for DREVEX) and caught the tax debtor false positive. Both correctly identify the key patterns (NACE mismatch, threshold gaming, buyer concentration) and properly apply CONFIRMED/INCONCLUSIVE/DISMISSED. The agent's advantage is schema compliance and the discovery of the Time & Data contract splitting pattern (absent from baseline). Content quality is the closest of any agent so far — this is where Opus shines regardless of instructions.

notes: Cross-referencer shows the SMALLEST delta of all agents (+23%) — reasoning quality is high with or without instructions
Schema compliance (outputs_valid_json, summary_with_risk_level) is the only consistent differentiator
Content assertions pass in BOTH configs: NACE pattern, micro capacity, threshold gaming, buyer concentration, innocent explanations
Baseline YEMANITY found 10 findings vs agent's 5 — MORE thorough but less focused. Includes subcontracting and geographic analysis
Baseline DREVEX caught the tax debtor false positive via name matching validation — excellent independent analysis
Agent discovered the Time & Data contract splitting finding that baseline missed — a genuine analytical win from reading critical-validation patterns
Both configs take ~3 minutes (Opus reasoning). Duration is comparable because the thinking dominates, not instruction reading
This is the agent where model choice matters most — Opus reasoning produces strong analysis regardless of instructions
